props.heading: Executive summary
props.text: Latin America and the Caribbean face a slowing economic outlook, with regional growth falling to 3.4 percent in 2022 and projected to drop to 2.8 percent in 2023 amid inflation, rate hikes, tightening fiscal positions, high debt, and spillovers from the war in Ukraine. Structural vulnerabilities—including rigid and informal labor markets and low productivity—left the region the slowest-growing globally from 2014–2019 and the worst affected economically by COVID-19 in 2020. Sustaining recovery and building inclusive, productive, and sustainable development over the long term will require private-sector engagement alongside public action. Drawing on surveys and interviews with senior executives at multinational firms operating in the region, the analysis identifies five opportunity areas: building human capital, advancing digital transformation, strengthening institutions and competitiveness, addressing multidimensional inequality, and meaningfully advancing the green agenda. Sixteen accompanying recommendations outline how companies can act, including addressing gender-based and place-based inequality, empowering SMEs, creating green jobs, promoting the circular economy, and partaking in green finance. The findings underscore the role of public-private cooperation and the multilateral sector in supporting the region's prosperity.
